name: Basic AMQ Spring JMS Consumer
helpText:
Basic AMQ Spring JMS Consumer

The JMS consumer is an Event Driven Consumer, used to connect to any Vendor specific JMS Broker(ActiveMQ, HornetQ, IBM MQ etc). However one need to include the related vendor specific libraries in the IM.
allowableProperties_comment: Note that the order of properties in this file dictates the order displayed on screen, unless propertyDisplayOrder is set, which will be used as the primary key

componentName.helpText: The name of the component as displayed on diagrams, spaces are encouraged, succinct is best. The name should be unique for the flow. The name will be used internally to reflect the bean created.

destinationJndiName.helpText:
Typically for Ikasan modules, the queue/topic e.g. dynamicQueues/myQName or dynamicTopics/myTopicName

However, if destinationJndiProperty* properties are set — it is used as a JNDI lookup name to resolve the Destination from the JNDI context (JndiUtils.getDestination(…)), same pattern as the connection factory lookup.

autoContentConversion.comments: This is not mandatory but should always be considered when creating this component
autoContentConversion.helpText:
Though the default for this configuration is false, it is more likely that this should be set to true.

autoContentConversion controls whether the consumer unwraps the JMS message envelope and passes just the payload content to the flow, or passes the raw javax.jms.Message object as-is.

Default: false — the raw JMS Message object flows through.

When true, these conversions are applied :

JMS Message TypePayload delivered to flow
TextMessageString
MapMessageMap<String, Object>
ObjectMessageThe unwrapped Object
BytesMessagebyte[]
StreamMessageRaw Message (passed through unchanged)
Anything elseRaw Message (passed through unchanged)

When to leave it false (the default):

Leave it false when you need access to JMS message headers or properties (e.g. message.getJMSCorrelationID(), message.getStringProperty(“someHeader”)) in your downstream components, since those are only available on the Message object itself. You would then use a JMS-aware converter (e.g. MapMessageToPayloadConverter) to extract what you need explicitly.

connectionFactoryJndiPropertyFactoryInit.helpText:
Sets the java.naming.factory.initial JNDI property in the application.properties file i.e. it tells the JNDI InitialContext which context factory class to use to look up the JMS ConnectionFactory.

For ActiveMQ you should always set it to org.apache.activemq.jndi.ActiveMQInitialContextFactory

Without FactoryInitial set, the JVM has no way to resolve the JNDI context and the connection factory lookup will fail at startup.

connectionFactoryJndiPropertyProviderUrl.helpText:
Sets the java.naming.provider.url JNDI property (Context.PROVIDER_URL). It tells the JNDI context where to find the AMQ broker so it can look up the ConnectionFactory. Typical values for ActiveMQ:

  • TCP (standard) - tcp://localhost:61616
  • Failover (HA) - failover:(tcp://broker1:61616,tcp://broker2:61616)
  • SSL - ssl://localhost:61617
  • Testing - vm://embedded-broker?create=true&broker.persistent=false&broker.useJmx=false

Important distinction: there are two separate sets of JNDI properties on the consumer:

  • connectionFactoryJndiProperty — used to look up the ConnectionFactory
  • destinationJndiProperty — used to look up the queue/topic destination

For AMQ both typically point to the same broker URL, so you set ProviderUrl on both:

  • setConnectionFactoryJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(jmsNamingFactoryInitial)
  • setConnectionFactoryJndiPropertyProviderUrl(brokerUrl)
  • setDestinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(jmsNamingFactoryInitial)
  • setDestinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl(brokerUrl)

connectionFactoryName.helpText:
For ActiveMQ the standard value is: ConnectionFactory

connectionFactoryName is the JNDI name used to look up the ConnectionFactory from the JNDI context that was set up via connectionFactoryJndiProperty*. It is passed directly to JndiUtils.getConnectionFactory(jndiProperties, connectionFactoryName) (IkasanMessageListenerContainer.java:118).

In plain terms: once the JNDI context knows where the broker is (via ProviderUrl) and how to talk to it (via FactoryInitial), connectionFactoryName is the name registered in that JNDI context under which the ConnectionFactory object can be retrieved.

If you change to a different broker or JNDI provider (e.g. a remote JNDI server), this name would need to match whatever that server registers the factory under.

pubSubDomain.comments: This is not mandatory but should always be considered when creating this component
pubSubDomain.helpText:
pubSubDomain

This is a boolean flag that controls whether the destination is a topic (true) or a queue (false). It defaults to false (queue).

What it does

The flag is used in two ways at consumer start-up (see IkasanMessageListenerContainer:153,190):

Destination creation — when resolving the destination directly from the broker session (no JNDI), it determines whether to call session.createTopic(name) or session.createQueue(name).
Spring container — it is passed to the underlying DefaultMessageListenerContainer.setPubSubDomain(), which tells Spring's JMS infrastructure how to treat the destination for all subsequent operations (acknowledgement, transactions, etc.).
Getting this wrong means your consumer will look for the wrong destination type and fail to connect, or silently consume from the wrong place.

Typical usage

// Consuming from a queue (default — can be omitted) .setPubSubDomain(false)

// Consuming from a topic .setPubSubDomain(true)

Interaction with durable subscriptions

When pubSubDomain is true (topic), you will typically also set:

.setPubSubDomain(true)
.setDurable(true) // survive flow restarts
.setDurableSubscriptionName("mySubName") // unique name for the durable subscription
Durable subscriptions only apply to topics — they have no meaning for queues.

Summary

Value	Destination type	Use when
false (default)	Queue	Point-to-point — one consumer receives each message
true	Topic	Publish-subscribe — all subscribers receive each message

sessionTransacted.comments: This is not mandatory but should always be considered when creating this component
sessionTransacted.helpText:
sessionTransacted

A boolean that controls whether the JMS session operates within a local transaction. It defaults to false.

What it does

It is passed directly to connection.createSession(sessionTransacted, acknowledgeMode) at consumer start-up.

When true — the broker holds the message until the session explicitly commits or rolls back. The message is only removed from the queue/topic on a successful commit.
When false — acknowledgement is controlled by sessionAcknowledgeMode instead (default AUTO_ACKNOWLEDGE), meaning the broker removes the message as soon as it is delivered.
Local transaction vs. XA/distributed transaction

This is a local JMS-only transaction — it only covers the JMS session itself. It is not the same as a distributed (XA) transaction coordinated by Arjuna/JTA, which spans both the JMS session and a database in a single atomic unit.

Setting	Transaction scope	Coordinator
sessionTransacted=true	JMS session only	AMQ broker
XA via JmsXA connection factory	JMS + DB (distributed)	Arjuna/JTA
Note: The arjuna consumer docs show sessionTransacted=true used alongside connectionFactoryName="java:/JmsXA" and an Arjuna transaction manager. In that setup the local session transaction is subordinate to the XA transaction.

Typical usage

Standard flow with redelivery on failure (most common):

.setSessionTransacted(true)

If the flow throws an exception the JMS session rolls back and the broker redelivers the message, preventing message loss.

Fire-and-forget / best-effort (lower overhead, acceptable message loss):

.setSessionTransacted(false) // default — can be omitted

The message is acknowledged immediately on delivery regardless of whether processing succeeds.

Relationship to sessionAcknowledgeMode

▎ When sessionTransacted is true, the sessionAcknowledgeMode value is effectively ignored by the JMS spec — acknowledgement is driven by commit/rollback, not the acknowledge mode. Only set sessionAcknowledgeMode when sessionTransacted is false.

configurable.helpText: This property reflects that a component has the necessary configuration setup so that it can be edited in the blue console. Its is more of a status and as such is not explicitly used.

configuredResourceId.helpText:
configuredResourceId is the unique key used to store and retrieve a component’s configuration from the Ikasan configuration database. It is the identity of the configuration — if two components share the same ID, they share the same persisted configuration.

This is defined by the ConfiguredResource interface (ConfiguredResource.java:55) and used by the framework at flow startup to load configuration from the DB (VisitingInvokerFlow.java):

If it is not set, the framework auto-generates one:

  • flowElement.getFlowComponent().setConfiguredResourceId(moduleName + flowName + componentName);

Why you should always set it explicitly:

  • If you want multiple components to all share the same configuration, use a meaningful shared key.
  • If you want to use a non-default naming convention, e.g. the flow name has changed, but you wish to keep any settings already configured in your production DB.

connectionFactoryPassword.helpText:
connectionFactoryUsername and connectionFactoryPassword

JMS broker authentication credentials, used when the consumer needs to authenticate with the AMQ broker to obtain a connection.

When the consumer starts, it must look up the ConnectionFactory from JNDI and establish a connection to the broker.

  • When connectionFactoryUsername is not set → looks up the connection factory via plain JNDI, no broker-level auth.
  • Otherwise → looks up an AuthenticatedConnectionFactory that calls connectionFactory.createConnection(username, password), authenticating the connection at the JMS level.

The connectionFactoryPassword field is annotated @Masked, so it is redacted in logs and the Ikasan dashboard UI.

Typical usage

Local/embedded ActiveMQ (dev) — usually omit both; the broker accepts unauthenticated connections by default.

Remote/secured ActiveMQ broker — set both to the credentials the broker requires:

Important distinction

These are broker-level connection credentials, not JNDI directory credentials. If you also need to authenticate with the JNDI directory to look up the connection factory, those credentials go into connectionFactoryJNDIProperties using the keys java.naming.security.principal and java.naming.security.credentials (see destinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal) — a separate concern.

PropertyPurpose
connectionFactoryUsernameUsername to authenticate the JMS connection with the broker
connectionFactoryPasswordPassword for that authentication (masked in UI/logs)
connectionFactoryJNDIProperties["java.naming.security.principal"]Username to authenticate with the JNDI directory

connectionFactoryUsername.helpText:
connectionFactoryUsername and connectionFactoryPassword

JMS broker authentication credentials, used when the consumer needs to authenticate with the AMQ broker to obtain a connection.

When the consumer starts, it must look up the ConnectionFactory from JNDI and establish a connection to the broker.

  • When connectionFactoryUsername is not set → looks up the connection factory via plain JNDI, no broker-level auth.
  • Otherwise → looks up an AuthenticatedConnectionFactory that calls connectionFactory.createConnection(username, password), authenticating the connection at the JMS level.

The connectionFactoryPassword field is annotated @Masked, so it is redacted in logs and the Ikasan dashboard UI.

Typical usage

Local/embedded ActiveMQ (dev) — usually omit both; the broker accepts unauthenticated connections by default.

Remote/secured ActiveMQ broker — set both to the credentials the broker requires:

Important distinction

These are broker-level connection credentials, not JNDI directory credentials. If you also need to authenticate with the JNDI directory to look up the connection factory, those credentials go into connectionFactoryJNDIProperties using the keys java.naming.security.principal and java.naming.security.credentials (see destinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal) — a separate concern.

PropertyPurpose
connectionFactoryUsernameUsername to authenticate the JMS connection with the broker
connectionFactoryPasswordPassword for that authentication (masked in UI/logs)
connectionFactoryJNDIProperties["java.naming.security.principal"]Username to authenticate with the JNDI directory

destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial.helpText:
You only need destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial when destinationJndiProperties is populated

This is a convenience setter that puts the standard JNDI property java.naming.factory.initial (i.e., Context.INITIAL_CONTEXT_FACTORY) into the destinationJndiProperties map.

What it does

When the consumer needs to look up the destination (queue/topic) via JNDI — as opposed to creating it directly from a session — it must bootstrap a javax.naming.InitialContext. The factory.initial property tells Java which InitialContextFactory class to instantiate to connect to that naming service. Without it, the JVM falls back to the default context (usually only works if you’re running inside an app server).

Note: there are two parallel sets of JNDI properties:

Property setUsed for
destinationJndiProperties (destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial)Looking up the queue/topic name
connectionFactoryJndiProperties (connectionFactoryJndiPropertyFactoryInitial)Looking up the ConnectionFactory

When you need it

You only need this when destinationJndiProperties is populated (i.e., the destination is resolved via remote JNDI lookup). If the destination name is resolved directly from the broker connection (the more common case with embedded or local ActiveMQ), the destinationJndiProperties map is empty and this property is ignored — the consumer creates the queue/topic via session.createQueue(name) instead.

Typical value for ActiveMQ

.setDestinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(“org.apache.activemq.jndi.ActiveMQInitialContextFactory”) .setDestinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl(“tcp://mybroker:61616”) // Optionally if the factory itself also needs JNDI lookup: .setConnectionFactoryJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(“org.apache.activemq.jndi.ActiveMQInitialContextFactory”) .setConnectionFactoryJndiPropertyProviderUrl(“tcp://mybroker:61616”)

For a WebLogic or other J2EE app server context it would be something like weblogic.jndi.WLInitialContextFactory.

Common pattern (from the codebase docs)

componentBuilder.jmsConsumer() .setDestinationJndiName(“jms/MyQueue”) .setConnectionFactoryName(“jms/ConnectionFactory”) .setConnectionFactoryJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(jmsNamingFactoryInitial) .setConnectionFactoryJndiPropertyProviderUrl(brokerUrl) .setDestinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(jmsNamingFactoryInitial) .setDestinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl(brokerUrl)

In short: it tells the JVM which JNDI provider class to use when resolving the destination name — only needed for remote/JNDI-based destination resolution.

destinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl.helpText:
destinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl is only required if destinationJndiProperties is set

This sets the standard JNDI property java.naming.provider.url (Context.PROVIDER_URL) in the destinationJndiProperties map. It is the network address of the naming/directory service used to look up the destination (queue/topic).

What it does

When the consumer resolves the destination via JNDI, this URL tells the InitialContext where to connect — i.e., the address of the broker (or JNDI server) that holds the destination binding. For ActiveMQ this is the broker's transport URL.

Relationship to destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial

These two always go together — factoryInitial says which class handles the lookup, providerUrl says where to connect:

Property	Role
destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial	The InitialContextFactory class to use
destinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl	The URL of the naming service to connect to
Typical usage

destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial "org.apache.activemq.jndi.ActiveMQInitialContextFactory"
destinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl "tcp://mybroker:61616"
In practice, both destinationJndiProperties and connectionFactoryJndiProperties usually point at the same broker URL, as seen in the sample module (ModuleConfig.java:77-80):

When you can omit it

If destinationJndiProperties is empty/null, the consumer skips the JNDI destination lookup entirely and resolves the queue/topic directly from the broker connection via session.createQueue(name). This is the simpler and more common approach with local or embedded ActiveMQ — in that case you don't need any of the destinationJndiProperty* settings.

destinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentia.helpText:
destinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentials is only required if destinationJndiProperties is populated.

This sets the standard JNDI property java.naming.security.credentials (Context.SECURITY_CREDENTIALS) in the destinationJndiProperties map. It is the password used to authenticate with the JNDI naming service when looking up the destination (queue/topic).

What it does

When the consumer performs a JNDI lookup to resolve the destination, the naming service may require authentication. This property provides the credential (password) for that authentication. It always pairs with destinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal, which provides the username.

Important distinction from connectionFactoryPassword

PropertyAuthenticates withPurpose
destinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentialsThe JNDI naming servicePassword to look up the destination name in the directory
connectionFactoryJndiPropertySecurityCredentialsThe JNDI naming servicePassword to look up the ConnectionFactory in the directory
connectionFactoryPasswordThe AMQ brokerPassword to open the JMS connection to the broker itself

Typical usage

In most ActiveMQ deployments the JNDI provider is the broker itself and does not require separate JNDI authentication — you can omit this property. It is primarily needed when connecting to a secured enterprise JNDI directory (e.g. WebLogic, LDAP-backed JNDI) that requires a login before it will serve lookups.

If you were to use this in Java code, it would typically look like

componentBuilder.jmsConsumer() .setDestinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial(“org.apache.activemq.jndi.ActiveMQInitialContextFactory”) .setDestinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl(“tcp://mybroker:61616”) .setDestinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal(“jndiUser”) .setDestinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentials(“jndiPassword”) // Broker-level authentication — a separate concern .setConnectionFactoryUsername(“brokerUser”) .setConnectionFactoryPassword(“brokerPassword”)

When you can omit it

If destinationJndiProperties is empty/null, the consumer skips JNDI destination resolution and creates the queue/topic directly via session.createQueue(name). In that case none of the destinationJndiProperty* settings are needed, including this one.

destinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal.helpText:
destinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal is only required if destinationJndiProperties is populated.

Sets the standard JNDI property java.naming.security.principal (Context.SECURITY_PRINCIPAL) in the destinationJndiProperties map. It is the username used to authenticate with the JNDI naming service when looking up the destination (queue/topic).

What it does

It pairs with destinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentials to form the username/password pair for JNDI directory authentication. Neither is useful without the other.

The full JNDI property set in context

The four destinationJndiProperty* setters each populate one entry in the destinationJndiProperties map:

Setter	JNDI key	Role
destinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial	java.naming.factory.initial	Which InitialContextFactory class to use
destinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl	java.naming.provider.url	Where the naming service is
destinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal	java.naming.security.principal	Username for the naming service
destinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentials	java.naming.security.credentials	Password for the naming service
Typical usage

If you were to code this in Java, it would typically look like

componentBuilder.jmsConsumer() .setDestinationJndiName("jms/MyQueue") .setDestinationJndiPropertyFactoryInitial("org.apache.activemq.jndi.ActiveMQInitialContextFactory") .setDestinationJndiPropertyProviderUrl("tcp://mybroker:61616") .setDestinationJndiPropertySecurityPrincipal("jndiUser") // username .setDestinationJndiPropertySecurityCredentials("jndiPassword") // password

When you can omit it

Same as securityCredentials — if destinationJndiProperties is empty/null, the consumer resolves the destination directly via session.createQueue(name) and none of these JNDI properties apply. For most local or embedded ActiveMQ setups, the JNDI directory doesn't require authentication and both securityPrincipal and securityCredentials can be left unset.
